item.title: Capitolul 1: Structura argumentului
item.dc:creator: demo-autor
item.description: Orice argument valid are trei componente: afirmația (ceea ce susții), dovada (ce demonstrează afirmația) și raționamentul (legătura logică dintre ele).

item.title: Chapter 1: The Anatomy of an Argument
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: Every argument, however complex, can be reduced to three components: a claim, the evidence that supports it, and the reasoning that connects the two. Understanding this structure is the prerequisite for everything else in this guide.

item.title: Capitolul 2: Sofisme frecvente
item.dc:creator: demo-autor
item.description: Sofismele sunt erori de raționament care par valide la prima vedere. Recunoașterea lor este esențială pentru evaluarea argumentelor.

item.title: Chapter 2: Evidence and Its Limits
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: Evidence does not speak for itself. It is selected, framed, and interpreted. The most important skill in evaluating arguments is not finding more evidence but assessing the quality of the evidence you already have.

item.title: Chapter 3: Fallacies and Misdirection
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: A fallacy is a reasoning error that makes an invalid argument look valid. Learning to recognise them is not about catching people out — it is about protecting your own thinking from patterns that feel compelling but do not actually work.

item.title: Chapter 4: Counter-Arguments and Steelmanning
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: The best preparation for making an argument is to understand, as deeply as possible, why a reasonable person might disagree with it. This is not courtesy. It is method.

item.title: Chapter 5: Writing the Argument
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: The argument exists in your head before it exists on the page, and the two versions are rarely identical. Writing is not the transcription of finished thinking — it is a stage in the development of the thinking itself.

item.title: Tag Taxonomy Standardised
item.description: All articles and announcements have been re-tagged using a standardised taxonomy. Old freeform tags have been mapped to the new controlled vocabulary.

item.title: Critical Thinking as Infrastructure
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: The capacity to evaluate arguments, detect fallacies, and construct coherent reasoning does not emerge spontaneously. It is a skill that must be taught, practised, and assessed — as systematically as mathematics or grammar.

item.title: Gândirea critică în educație
item.dc:creator: demo-autor
item.description: Capacitatea de a evalua argumente, de a detecta sofisme și de a construi raționamente coerente nu apare spontan. Este o abilitate care se predă, se exersează și se evaluează — la fel ca matematica sau literatura.

item.title: Open Call for Contributors — Summer 2026
item.description: Academis Demo is open to new contributors. We are looking for writers with strong analytical voices in history, technology, urban policy, and media criticism. Work is published under CC BY 4.0.

item.title: Contributor Guide Updated — Version 2
item.description: The contributor guide has been substantially revised to clarify pitch format requirements, our review timeline, and what editors look for in a strong argument pitch.

item.title: Influența otomană în Principatele Române
item.dc:creator: demo-autor
item.description: Această lucrare examinează mecanismele prin care Principatele Valahia și Moldova au menținut o autonomie administrativă substanțială în cadrul suzeranității otomane, comparativ cu provinciile direct anexate Imperiului.

item.title: New Series: Cities, Data, and the Public Interest
item.description: A new editorial series exploring the intersection of urban infrastructure, digital governance, and civic imagination. The first two essays are live; three more are in preparation.

item.title: The Civic Cost of Platform Monopolies
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: When two or three corporations control the default pathways through which most citizens encounter information, the question is no longer merely economic. It is political, epistemic, and ultimately constitutional.

item.title: Editorial Board Formed — April 2026
item.description: Academis Demo has established a four-person editorial board to provide oversight of commissioning decisions and to handle appeals from contributors.

item.title: The Unfinished Argument for Urban Density
item.dc:creator: thomas-vance
item.description: The empirical case for urban density is strong and largely settled. It reduces carbon emissions, shortens commutes, lowers infrastructure costs, and correlates with better health outcomes. And yet density-positive policy consistently fails at the ballot box. The problem is not the evidence. It is the argument.

item.title: CC BY 4.0 Licence Adopted for All Content
item.description: All Academis Demo publications are now released under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International. Existing articles have been retroactively relicensed.

item.title: Paper Reading Workshop — Spring 2026
item.description: A six-session workshop on close reading of academic papers, held online every other Tuesday. Open to contributors and readers.

item.title: Program de dezbateri academice
item.dc:creator: demo-autor
item.description: Proiectarea și implementarea unui curriculum de dezbateri academice pentru elevi de liceu, cu focus pe argumentare structurată, gândire critică și comunicare publică.

item.title: Symposium Report: Evidence in Public Discourse — February 2026
item.description: A brief account of the one-day symposium held in February 2026 on the use and misuse of evidence in journalism, policy, and academic publishing.

item.title: The Science-to-Headline Pipeline and Where It Breaks
item.dc:creator: mara-quinn
item.description: By the time a scientific finding appears in a general-audience headline, it has passed through a series of transformations — each individually explainable, collectively damaging — that typically leave the original result barely recognisable. This is not a problem of bad actors. It is a problem of misaligned incentives at every stage of the process.

item.title: Corrections Policy Published
item.description: We have published a formal corrections policy covering factual errors, methodological disputes, and retraction criteria.

item.title: RSS Feed Now Available
item.description: Academis Demo now publishes a full-content RSS feed. Subscribe to receive every new article and announcement in your reader of choice.

item.title: Why Evidence Hierarchies Break Down in Policy Debates
item.dc:creator: mara-quinn
item.description: The hierarchy of evidence — RCT at the top, expert opinion at the bottom — is a useful heuristic within clinical medicine. Applied uncritically to policy, it produces both overconfidence in what we know and systematic blindness to what we cannot measure.

item.title: What Translation Loses and What That Means for History
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: Every historian who works with sources in languages they cannot fully read is doing epistemology under conditions of incomplete information. This is not a methodological failure — it is a condition of the discipline. But it is more consequential than most historical writing acknowledges.

item.title: Print and the Disruption of Epistemic Authority in Sixteenth-Century Europe
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: This article examines how the rapid proliferation of print between 1517 and 1618 destabilised the mechanisms through which epistemic authority was assigned, contested, and reconstructed in the Holy Roman Empire, France, and England. The argument is that the printing press was not merely a neutral dissemination technology but a structural change in the information market.

item.title: Building an Editorial Style Guide for Independent Publications
item.dc:creator: mara-quinn
item.description: A style guide is not a list of spelling preferences. It is a record of editorial decisions — what the publication believes about language, evidence, argument, and its obligations to readers. Most independent publications either have no guide at all or mistake a usage glossary for one.

item.title: Transparency Requirements for Automated Decision Systems in Public Administration
item.dc:creator: thomas-vance
item.description: As public authorities increasingly delegate consequential decisions to automated systems — benefits eligibility, risk scoring, planning approvals — the question of accountability becomes structurally different from the accountability questions we know how to answer. This article maps current regulatory responses and identifies three persistent gaps.

item.title: Inaugural Issue — Call for Submissions
item.description: The first open call for contributions to Academis Demo. We received 34 pitches and commissioned 8 pieces. Submissions are now closed.

item.title: Civic Memory Archive
item.dc:creator: thomas-vance
item.description: An eighteen-month project to systematically document, structure, and publish the physical evidence of four decades of informal urban adaptation in a former industrial district — before demolition makes the record impossible.

item.title: Open Urban Documentation Initiative
item.dc:creator: thomas-vance
item.description: A two-year project to document, archive, and publish the built fabric of informal housing settlements in three European post-industrial cities — creating a resource that is free, searchable, and maintained by the communities it documents.
